name: River Crossing
notes: Classic wolf-goat-cabbage variant

name: Tower of Hanoi
notes: Recursive puzzle with 2^n - 1 minimum moves

name: Sudoku (Easy)
notes: 9x9 grid, no repeats in row/col/box

name: Wordle
notes: 5-letter word in 6 guesses with color hints

name: Tangram
notes: Arrange 7 shapes to form a given silhouette

name: Cryptogram
notes: Substitution cipher of a quote

name: KenKen (4x4)
notes: Like sudoku but with arithmetic cage constraints

name: Lateral Thinking Scenario
notes: Yes/no question format, great for groups

name: Nonogram (10x10)
notes: Shade cells by row/column number clues to reveal picture

name: Rubik's Cube
notes: 3x3 classic; beginner method ~8 algorithms

name: Einstein's Riddle
notes: 5 houses, 5 attributes, deduction grid recommended

name: Crossword (Mon/Tue)
notes: Early-week NYT crosswords are beginner-friendly

name: Soma Cube
notes: 240 distinct ways to assemble 7 pieces into 3x3x3

name: Kakuro
notes: Crossword-style grid with sum clues, digits 1-9 no repeats

name: Prisoners' Hat Problem
notes: Classic info theory puzzle, involves parity strategy

name: Spot the Difference
notes: Find N differences between two nearly identical images

name: Connections
notes: Group 16 words into 4 categories of 4

name: Mastermind
notes: Deduce 4-peg code in ≤10 guesses using black/white hints

name: Bridges (Hashiwokakero)
notes: Connect islands with bridges matching each island's number

name: Twenty Questions
notes: Binary search strategy; great icebreaker for groups

name: Nonogram
notes: Shade cells in a grid according to row/column number clues to reveal a hidden picture.

name: Cryptic Crossword
notes: Each clue is a wordplay puzzle in itself, combining anagrams, hidden words, double definitions, and other devices.

name: Slitherlink
notes: Draw a single closed loop along grid edges so that numbered cells indicate exactly how many of their sides are part of the loop.

name: Nurikabe
notes: Shade cells to create a connected 'sea' with no 2x2 blocks, leaving numbered islands of the given size.

name: Masyu
notes: Draw a single loop through cells; the loop must turn on black circles and go straight through white circles (with turns on at least one adja

name: Rush Hour
notes: Slide blocking vehicles on a 6x6 grid to clear a path for the target car to exit.

name: Hitori
notes: Shade duplicate numbers in rows/columns so no duplicates remain; shaded cells cannot touch, and unshaded cells must form one connected group

name: Logic Grid Puzzle
notes: Given a set of clues, use a cross-referencing grid to deduce which attributes belong to which entity (e.g. Einstein's riddle style).

name: Pentomino Tiling
notes: Fit all 12 pentomino shapes into a rectangular or irregular region with no gaps or overlaps.

name: Hashiwokakero (Bridges)
notes: Connect islands (numbered circles) with horizontal/vertical bridges so each island's number matches its bridge count and all islands are con

name: Sokoban
notes: Push all crates onto goal squares in a warehouse grid; crates can only be pushed (not pulled) and cannot pass through walls or each other.

name: Light Up (Akari)
notes: Place light bulbs in white cells so every white cell is illuminated; no two bulbs may shine on each other, and numbered walls indicate adjac

name: Picross 3D
notes: Break away blocks from a 3D rectangular solid using number clues on rows/columns/depth to reveal a hidden 3D object.

name: Zebra Puzzle
notes: Classic constraint-satisfaction riddle: determine who owns the zebra and who drinks water given 15 clues about 5 houses.

name: Star Battle
notes: Place stars in a grid so that each row, column, and bold-outlined region contains exactly the required number of stars, with no stars touchi

name: Cross Sums (Kakurasu)
notes: Shade cells in a grid so the sum of shaded cell positions in each row/column matches the given clue values.

name: Burr Puzzle
notes: Disassemble and reassemble notched interlocking wooden pieces; the classic 6-piece burr requires finding the key piece to unlock the structu

notes: Fill every cell with a number so that connected groups of the same number have exactly that many cells; same-number groups may not share an
